immediate_actions: Apply direct pressure with clean cloth to control bleeding
Do not remove any impaled objects
Elevate injured limb if possible
steps_community_health_post: Wash hands with soap before touching wound
Irrigate wound thoroughly with clean water or normal saline for at least 5 minutes
Remove visible debris gently with forceps
Apply antiseptic (chlorhexidine 0.05% or povidone iodine)
For wounds less than 2cm: close with steri-strips and cover with sterile dressing
Change dressing every 24-48 hours
Give paracetamol 500mg for pain relief
Check tetanus immunization status — give TT booster if not immunized in 5 years
refer_if: Wound on face, hand, or joint
Signs of infection: redness, swelling, pus, fever after 24-48 hours
Bleeding not controlled after 10 minutes of direct pressure
Deep wound possibly involving tendon, bone or nerve

immediate_actions: Take temperature
If temperature above 38.5°C give paracetamol
Remove excess clothing
Encourage fluids
steps_community_health_post: Measure temperature (axillary: add 0.5°C for true temperature)
Assess for malaria if in endemic area — do RDT if available
Give paracetamol 15mg/kg every 4-6 hours
Encourage ORS or breastfeeding if still breastfed
Assess for danger signs: convulsion, stiff neck, severe vomiting, not drinking
Tepid sponging if temperature above 39°C
Advise caregiver to return immediately if child becomes worse
steps_district_clinic: Consider lumbar puncture if meningitis suspected
refer_if: Temperature above 39.5°C not responding to paracetamol
Child under 2 months with any fever
fever_child.source: WHO IMCI Chart Booklet 2024, WHO ETAT Guidelines

immediate_actions: Test with RDT (Rapid Diagnostic Test) if available
Give paracetamol for fever while awaiting diagnosis
Assess for danger signs
steps_community_health_post: Confirm positive malaria RDT or clinical diagnosis in endemic area
Give artemether-lumefantrine (AL) based on weight
Ensure first dose taken with food
Explain 6-dose schedule over 3 days
Give paracetamol for fever
Advise to return if no improvement in 48 hours or any danger sign
Assess nutrition and give vitamin A if eligible
steps_district_clinic: Treat severe malaria with IV artesunate
refer_if: Danger signs: convulsion, altered consciousness, inability to drink
Severe anaemia (very pale palms, severe weakness)
No improvement after 48 hours of treatment
malaria_uncomplicated.source: WHO Guidelines for Treatment of Malaria 2022

immediate_actions: REFER IMMEDIATELY to hospital
Give rectal artesunate 10mg/kg if available while arranging transport
Do not delay referral for any reason
Prevent hypoglycaemia: give sugar solution if patient can swallow
steps_community_health_post: Give pre-referral rectal artesunate if available
Manage hypoglycaemia with sugar solution orally if conscious
Prevent convulsions: do not leave patient alone
Transport immediately to hospital
steps_district_clinic: IV artesunate 2.4mg/kg at 0, 12, 24 hours then daily
IV fluids for dehydration — carefully (risk of cerebral oedema)
refer_if: Always refer — this IS the referral case
danger_signs: Two or more convulsions in 24 hours
severe_malaria.source: WHO Guidelines for Treatment of Malaria 2022 — Severe Malaria

immediate_actions: Assess dehydration status
Start ORS immediately for any dehydration
Continue breastfeeding if breastfed
steps_community_health_post: Assess dehydration: skin turgor, sunken eyes, thirst, lethargy
Give ORS for mild-moderate dehydration: 50-100mL/kg over 3-4 hours
Give zinc: 10mg/day under 6 months; 20mg/day over 6 months for 10-14 days
Continue feeding — do not stop food
Advise on preparation of ORS and hygiene
Reassess after 3-4 hours of ORS
steps_district_clinic: IV fluids if unable to drink
Stool culture if bloody diarrhoea or cholera suspected
Antibiotics only for cholera or shigella (bloody diarrhoea)
refer_if: Signs of severe dehydration: sunken eyes, skin pinch returns slowly, unable to drink, lethargic
Bloody diarrhoea (dysentery) — treat with antibiotics and refer
diarrhoea_acute.source: WHO IMCI Guidelines 2024, WHO Diarrhoea Treatment Guidelines

immediate_actions: Count respiratory rate for one full minute
Check for chest indrawing
Assess oxygen saturation if pulse oximeter available
Give first dose of antibiotic immediately if pneumonia confirmed
steps_community_health_post: Measure respiratory rate: fast breathing = over 60/min under 2 months; over 50/min 2-11 months; over 40/min 1-5 years
Look for chest indrawing, grunting, nasal flaring
Classify: fast breathing without indrawing = non-severe pneumonia
For non-severe: amoxicillin 25mg/kg twice daily for 5 days
Give paracetamol for fever
Encourage fluids and continued feeding
Advise to return immediately if breathing worsens, child cannot drink, or develops chest indrawing
steps_district_clinic: IV amoxicillin or ampicillin for severe pneumonia
refer_if: Under 2 months with any fast breathing

immediate_actions: Assess severity: limited redness vs spreading vs systemic symptoms
Check for abscess formation
steps_community_health_post: Clean wound with chlorhexidine or povidone iodine
Give amoxicillin 500mg every 8 hours for 7 days for mild cellulitis
Elevate affected limb
Mark edge of redness with pen to monitor spread
Give paracetamol for pain and fever
Review in 24-48 hours
refer_if: Redness spreading beyond marked border in 24 hours
Fluctuant abscess needing incision and drainage
No improvement in 48 hours of antibiotics

immediate_actions: Assess degree of dehydration
Start ORS immediately for any dehydration
Do not give plain water alone
steps_community_health_post: Mild dehydration (some signs): give ORS 50mL/kg over 3-4 hours
Reassess every hour
Continue ORS at 10-20mL/kg after each loose stool
Encourage breastfeeding and food
Teach mother/caregiver how to prepare and give ORS
steps_district_clinic: Severe dehydration: IV Ringer's lactate 100mL/kg over 3 hours (child) or 30mL/kg over 30 min then 70mL/kg over 2.5 hours
refer_if: Severe dehydration: sunken eyes, skin pinch slow, unable to drink, lethargic

immediate_actions: Check for bilateral pitting oedema
Check MUAC (mid-upper arm circumference)
Assess for medical complications
steps_community_health_post: MUAC under 115mm in child 6-59 months = SAM
Check for bilateral oedema
Screen for medical complications: diarrhoea, pneumonia, fever
Enrol in community-based management of acute malnutrition (CMAM) program
Give ready-to-use therapeutic food (RUTF) if available
Treat with amoxicillin 80mg/kg/day for 7 days
Give vitamin A
Treat any infections
refer_if: Any danger sign: unconscious, convulsion, respiratory distress
Medical complications: severe pneumonia, severe dehydration
malnutrition_severe.source: WHO Guidelines for the Management of Severe Acute Malnutrition 2023

immediate_actions: Assess for visual loss
Check if one or both eyes affected
Look for corneal clouding (emergency sign)
steps_community_health_post: Clean discharge from eye with clean wet cloth, from inner to outer corner
For bacterial conjunctivitis: apply tetracycline 1% eye ointment 3 times daily for 7 days
Neonatal conjunctivitis: apply tetracycline 1% ointment 3 times daily for 7 days
Advise good hand hygiene to prevent spread
Do not patch the eye
refer_if: No improvement after 3 days of treatment

immediate_actions: Ask about ear pain
Check for discharge from ear
Look behind ear for swelling (mastoiditis)
steps_community_health_post: Give amoxicillin 40mg/kg/day in 2 doses for 5 days
Give paracetamol for pain relief
Dry mopping of ear if discharge present with dry wick
Do not put anything in ear canal
Advise to complete full antibiotic course
refer_if: No improvement after 48 hours of antibiotics

immediate_actions: Assess for anaphylaxis or severe allergic reaction
Check for fever
Identify distribution: localised vs widespread
steps_community_health_post: Describe rash: macules, papules, vesicles, pustules, urticaria
Localised with no fever: consider contact dermatitis, insect bite, ringworm
Ringworm (tinea): clotrimazole cream twice daily for 2-4 weeks
Urticaria (hives): chlorphenamine 4mg (adult) or 0.1mg/kg (child)
Scabies: apply permethrin 5% cream to whole body overnight
Check vaccination history if measles rash suspected
refer_if: Widespread rash with fever — possible measles, dengue, typhoid
Purpuric rash (does not blanch on pressure — emergency)
Anaphylaxis: rash with breathing difficulty or collapse

immediate_actions: Check gestational age
Check blood pressure
Ask about vaginal bleeding or severe abdominal pain
steps_community_health_post: Measure blood pressure at every visit
Check fetal movement after 28 weeks
Give iron + folic acid supplementation throughout pregnancy
Give intermittent preventive treatment for malaria if in endemic area
Counsel on danger signs in pregnancy
Ensure antenatal care registration
steps_district_clinic: Ultrasound for dating and fetal wellbeing
refer_if: Headache with visual disturbance in pregnancy

immediate_actions: CALL FOR HELP IMMEDIATELY
Give misoprostol 600mcg orally or oxytocin 10 IU IM
Massage uterine fundus firmly
Prepare for immediate transfer to hospital
steps_community_health_post: Give uterotonic: misoprostol 600mcg orally (3 tablets) OR oxytocin 10IU IM
Bimanual uterine compression if trained
Establish IV access if possible
Keep patient warm
Elevate legs
Arrange emergency transport NOW
steps_district_clinic: IV oxytocin 20-40 IU in 1L normal saline
Surgical intervention: manual removal of placenta, repair of tears
refer_if: Always refer after initial treatment — this is always an emergency
postpartum_haemorrhage.source: WHO Guidelines for Management of Postpartum Haemorrhage 2023

immediate_actions: Ask about cough duration — 2+ weeks is TB suspect
Do not isolate patient without evidence, but advise cough hygiene
Note night sweats, weight loss, blood in sputum
steps_community_health_post: Identify TB symptoms: cough 2+ weeks, fever, night sweats, weight loss
Ask about contact with known TB case
Collect 2 sputum samples on 2 consecutive days
Refer for sputum microscopy or GeneXpert
Check HIV status
Do not start TB treatment without laboratory confirmation
Counsel on infection control: cover cough, ventilate rooms
steps_district_clinic: Start standard 6-month DOTS treatment if confirmed
refer_if: All suspected TB — must be confirmed at clinic level

immediate_actions: Keep patient calm and still — immobilize bitten limb
Do NOT cut wound, suck venom, or apply tourniquet
Remove rings, watches from bitten limb
REFER TO HOSPITAL IMMEDIATELY for antivenom
steps_community_health_post: Identify snake if safe — do not approach or kill snake (photograph if possible)
Immobilize limb with splint at heart level
Clean bite site with soap and water
Mark swelling border with pen and time
Monitor for systemic signs: drooping eyelids, difficulty swallowing, bleeding, paralysis
Arrange urgent transport to hospital with antivenom
steps_district_clinic: Specific antivenom based on snake species
Supportive care: IV fluids, airway management
refer_if: All snake bites should be referred for observation
Any systemic symptoms: collapse, bleeding, paralysis, ptosis, difficulty breathing
snake_bite.source: WHO Guidelines for Management of Snakebite 2019

immediate_actions: Stop the burning: remove from source, cool with running cool water for 20 minutes
Do NOT use ice, butter, toothpaste, or traditional remedies
Remove clothing and jewellery from burned area if not stuck
steps_community_health_post: Estimate burn surface area using palm method (patient's palm = 1%)
Cool burn with running clean water for 20 minutes
Cover with clean non-fluffy dressing
Give paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain
Give ORS or fluids by mouth
Minor burns under 5% body surface area without involvement of face/hands/genitals: manage locally
Apply clean dressing daily
steps_district_clinic: IV fluid resuscitation for major burns
refer_if: Burns covering more than 10% body surface (5% in children)
Burns to face, hands, feet, genitals, or major joints
Airway involvement: singed nose hairs, hoarse voice after fire
Child under 2 years with any significant burn

immediate_actions: Measure blood pressure twice — 5 minutes apart
Check for hypertensive emergency: headache, visual changes, chest pain, neuro symptoms
steps_community_health_post: Blood pressure above 140/90 on 2 readings = hypertension
Ask about symptoms of end-organ damage
Lifestyle advice: reduce salt, exercise, no tobacco, limit alcohol
Record and refer for initiation of antihypertensive medication
If already on medication: check adherence, refill prescription
steps_district_clinic: Initiate antihypertensive: amlodipine 5mg or hydrochlorothiazide or lisinopril
refer_if: Blood pressure above 140/90 with headache, visual changes, chest pain — EMERGENCY

immediate_actions: If patient conscious: give sugar immediately — 3 teaspoons sugar in water, glucose tablets, or sweet drink
If unconscious: DO NOT give anything by mouth — refer IMMEDIATELY
steps_community_health_post: Measure blood glucose if glucometer available (below 3.9mmol/L = hypoglycaemia)
If conscious: give 15-20g carbohydrate (3 tsp sugar in water, juice, glucose tablets)
Recheck in 15 minutes — repeat if still symptomatic
Once recovered: give snack or meal
Identify cause: missed meal, too much insulin/medication, exercise
Adjust medication or meal timing if recurrent
steps_district_clinic: Glucagon IM if IV access impossible
refer_if: Does not recover after 2 doses of oral sugar
diabetes_hypoglycaemia.source: WHO Global Diabetes Compact 2023, SIGN Guidelines

immediate_actions: Sit patient upright
Give salbutamol inhaler 4-8 puffs via spacer (or nebuliser if available)
Assess response in 15-20 minutes
steps_community_health_post: Assess severity: can patient speak in sentences? respiratory rate? accessory muscle use?
Mild-moderate: salbutamol 4-8 puffs via spacer every 20 minutes for 3 doses
If improving: continue salbutamol every 4-6 hours
Give prednisolone 1mg/kg (max 40mg) for 3-5 days if moderate attack
Identify and remove trigger if possible
Educate on inhaler technique
refer_if: Unable to speak in words — only words or silent chest (life-threatening)
No improvement after 3 doses of salbutamol
asthma_acute.source: WHO Package of Essential Non-communicable Disease Interventions 2023, GINA Guidelines

immediate_actions: Stay calm
Place child on side (recovery position) to prevent aspiration
Do not put anything in the mouth
Time the seizure
steps_community_health_post: Ensure airway is open: tilt head, lift chin
Place in recovery position on their side
Do not restrain movements
Give rectal diazepam 0.3mg/kg if available and seizure ongoing after 5 minutes
Reduce fever: paracetamol 15mg/kg once seizure stops
Reassure parents — febrile convulsions are common and usually not dangerous
Explain that child may be sleepy after seizure — this is normal
refer_if: Child does not return to normal within 30 minutes
Under 6 months or over 5 years with first febrile seizure
convulsion_febrile.source: WHO IMCI 2024, NICE Febrile Seizure Guidelines

immediate_actions: Assess severity: pallor of palms, tongue, conjunctiva
Check for severe anaemia: very pale palms with any fast breathing = emergency
steps_community_health_post: Assess pallor of palms, inner lower eyelid, tongue
Ask about diet, menstrual bleeding, worm symptoms
Give ferrous sulfate: adults 200mg 3x/day; children 3mg/kg/day elemental iron
Give folic acid alongside iron
Give mebendazole or albendazole for deworming
Advise on iron-rich foods
Treat malaria if positive
steps_district_clinic: Full blood count and blood film
Haemoglobin electrophoresis for suspected sickle cell
Blood transfusion for Hb below 5g/dL or any severe anaemia with symptoms
refer_if: Severe anaemia: very pale palms with fast breathing, lethargic, or chest pain
Not responding to iron after 4 weeks
anaemia.source: WHO Guidelines for Prevention and Management of Anaemia 2023

immediate_actions: EMERGENCY — newborns with danger signs need immediate referral
Keep baby warm — skin-to-skin with mother if possible
Ensure airway is clear
Give first dose of IM ampicillin and gentamicin if available before referral
steps_community_health_post: Look for danger signs in first 7 days of life
Keep baby warm: skin-to-skin, dry and covered
Encourage breastfeeding
Give IM benzylpenicillin 50000 IU/kg if neonatal sepsis suspected
Refer immediately to hospital with baby kept warm during transport
steps_district_clinic: IV ampicillin and gentamicin for neonatal sepsis
danger_signs: Pus from umbilicus with redness spreading to skin
Jaundice in first 24 hours or involving palms and soles
refer_if: Any danger sign — refer ALL sick newborns immediately

immediate_actions: Ask about fever — fever with UTI symptoms suggests kidney infection
Check for loin pain (upper UTI)
steps_community_health_post: Uncomplicated UTI (lower): dysuria, frequency, no fever
Give cotrimoxazole 960mg twice daily for 3 days (adults)
Or nitrofurantoin 100mg twice daily for 5 days if available
Encourage fluids — at least 2L/day
Advise personal hygiene
Urine dipstick if available: nitrites and leucocytes positive
refer_if: No improvement after 3 days of antibiotics
